title: Pemmican Ratios
description: Explore the science and tradition behind pemmican — the concentrated food that sustained Cree and Métis peoples for millennia. Learn the critical ratios and temperature requirements that make pemmican either a long-lasting nutritional powerhouse or a dangerous spoiled product. This session covers the principles; making actual pemmican requires proper equipment, ingredients, and attention to the temperature requirements presented here.

steps.instruction: What is pemmican: pemmican is a concentrated, shelf-stable food made from three core components — lean dried meat (traditionally bison, deer, or caribou), rendered fat (traditionally bison tallow or bear fat), and dried berries (traditionally saskatoon berries, chokecherries, or cranberries). At roughly 1:1:1 ratio by weight, pemmican provides complete protein, fat, and carbohydrates in a dense, portable form that can sustain heavy physical activity indefinitely.
steps.culturalContext: Pemmican is a Cree and Métis innovation that provided complete nutrition for long journeys, winter camps, and harsh seasons. The word 'pemmican' comes from the Cree word 'pimîhkân,' meaning 'manufactured grease.' Cree and Métis peoples refined this technology over generations, creating a food that could sustain life for months without other food sources. During the fur trade era, pemmican became so critical to the North West Company's supply chain that it was called 'the fuel of empire' — a recognition of Cree and Métis intellectual contribution to what became a continent-spanning enterprise.

steps.instruction: Lean meat selection and preparation: start with lean cuts — bison round, venison hindquarter, or caribou — with as much fat and connective tissue removed as possible. Fat in the meat itself will interfere with drying and cause premature spoilage. Slice meat across the grain into thin strips (1/4 inch or less). The goal is maximum surface area for rapid, complete drying.
steps.safetyNote: Meat must be fully dried before mixing with rendered fat. Residual moisture in insufficiently dried meat will promote bacterial growth even after mixing with fat. Properly dried meat will be brittle, hard, and snap cleanly. Any flexibility or chewiness indicates incomplete drying — continue until fully desiccated.
steps.culturalContext: Traditional Cree and Métis drying used sun and wind — meat hung on drying racks in warm, breezy conditions. The timing was seasonal: late summer and early fall provided optimal drying conditions before winter. Modern equivalents include low-temperature oven drying (170°F/77°C with the door slightly ajar) or food dehydrators set to 160°F/71°C.

steps.instruction: Fat rendering — CRITICAL temperature requirement: select hard fat (bison tallow, beef suet, or bear fat — not soft fat like pork lard, which has different rendering characteristics). Cut fat into small pieces and render in a heavy pot over medium heat, stirring frequently. The fat must reach and hold a minimum temperature of 250°F/121°C to eliminate bacterial pathogens. Use a thermometer — do not estimate by appearance.
steps.safetyNote: CRITICAL: Fat MUST be rendered to minimum 250°F/121°C and held for at least 5 minutes at that temperature to eliminate Listeria, Salmonella, E. coli, and other pathogens. Under-rendered fat will spoil within days, even when mixed with dried meat, and can cause serious illness. Under-rendered fat appears liquid and looks similar to properly rendered fat — you cannot judge doneness by appearance. A thermometer is not optional. After rendering, strain out all solid pieces through cheesecloth — solids harbor moisture and bacteria.
steps.culturalContext: Traditional rendering was done over open fire, which naturally achieved high temperatures. The transition to controlled cooking environments (stoves) means practitioners must be more intentional about reaching temperature thresholds that were automatic in traditional open-fire rendering.

steps.instruction: Berry preparation: select fully dried berries — saskatoon (serviceberries), chokecherries, highbush cranberries, or dried blueberries. Berries must be completely desiccated (no moisture). Pit cherries before drying. Berries can be left whole or roughly crushed; crushing releases oils and sugars that integrate more fully with the fat. Traditional ratio is roughly equal parts (by weight) dried meat, rendered fat, and dried berries.
steps.culturalContext: The choice of berries varied by region and season. Cree and Métis communities used locally available berries, which changed the flavor profile and nutritional content of regional pemmican varieties. Saskatoon berries (Amelanchier alnifolia) were particularly prized for their high sugar content and flavor. Berries contribute carbohydrates, vitamin C, and antioxidants that complement the protein and fat of the meat and tallow.

steps.instruction: Mixing and forming: while the rendered fat is still liquid (but cooled slightly from peak temperature — around 150-160°F/65-71°C), combine with dried meat and dried berries in a ratio of approximately 1:1:1 by weight. Mix thoroughly until all meat is coated with fat and berries are evenly distributed. Pour into molds (traditionally rawhide bags; modern equivalents: silicone molds, small containers) and allow to cool completely at room temperature until solid.
steps.safetyNote: Work quickly during mixing — the fat begins to solidify as it cools. Ensure all meat is fully coated with fat; exposed meat surfaces can develop mold during storage. Avoid adding any additional moisture (wet hands, damp equipment) during mixing.
steps.culturalContext: Traditional pemmican was formed in rawhide bags (parfleches) — the hide itself contributed to preservation through natural oils and tannins. Pemmican cakes could be stacked and transported easily. Voyageurs and fur traders relied on individual pemmican cakes that could be eaten without preparation — the original energy bar.

steps.instruction: Storage principles: properly made pemmican stores in cool, dry, dark conditions for 1-5 years. Store at room temperature (below 70°F/21°C) or refrigerated. Do not expose to moisture. Inspect periodically for off odors (rancid, sour, or unusual smells) or visible mold. Properly made pemmican has a mild savory fat-and-meat smell; any deviation from this profile warrants inspection.
steps.safetyNote: Properly made pemmican can store for years; improperly made pemmican — with insufficiently dried meat, under-rendered fat, or added moisture — can be dangerous within days. The difference is entirely in following the temperature and drying requirements precisely. If any step was uncertain (fat temperature not verified, meat drying time insufficient), consume within 2 weeks and refrigerate. When in doubt about the safety of homemade pemmican, discard.
steps.culturalContext: The long storage life of properly made pemmican was one of its most important properties — it enabled Cree and Métis peoples to prepare for winter shortfalls and to sustain themselves on long hunting and trading journeys. Pemmican caches stored in cold locations could sustain communities through unexpected hardship. This strategic food security function meant that pemmican-making knowledge was deeply valued — the knowledge keepers who could make reliable, long-lasting pemmican contributed directly to community survival.
